learning_outcomes: I can strengthen my reading comprehension and text decoding skills (skimming, scanning, mental models, summarising etc.)
I can understand the form of short stories and writer’s craft.
key_knowledge_components: To know how to strengthen reading comprehension and text decoding skills (skimming, scanning, summarising etc.)
To know an awareness of the short story form and writer’s craft.
extracted.suggested_activities_summary: Comprehension and analysing the writer’s craft: Check students understand the difference between skimming and scanning. Encourage students in pairs to discuss what they think the story is about after skim reading ‘The Things We Ate’. Monitor and encourage discussion of ‘powerful’ or ‘intriguing’ words and encourage students to add them to the word bank. Oracy: Discuss findings as a class Activity 1: Students scan the text to create a quick food …

learning_outcomes: I can explain how the writer methodically tracks characters/ themes and how/why these shift/ develop
I can develop oracy and writing skills and have more confidence in making judgements about a text.
key_knowledge_components: To know how the writer methodically tracks characters/ themes and how/why these shift/ develop
To know how to develop oracy and writing skills and have more confidence in making judgements about a text.

Encourage wider discussion about hybrid or mixed heritage and ideas about i…

learning_outcomes: I can define the terms ‘epistolary’ and ‘personification’
I can analyse their effect in the story.
key_knowledge_components: To know how the writer uses the epistolary form and personification to engage the reader.
extracted.suggested_activities_summary: Personification, form and creative writing: Personification activity and video Pre-reading vocabulary: wailing, brutality,concept, insignificant Students skim read the short story (2 minutes) ORACY: discuss what they think this story is about, gathering three ideas to share with others. Students read the story and highlight any examples of personification. They underline any unfamiliar words.

learning_outcomes: I can understand a writer’s craft by commenting on sentences, punctuation and grammar.
key_knowledge_components: To know how sentences and unpicking the punctuation/ grammar affects the reader’s interpretation of a text.
extracted.suggested_activities_summary: Reading comprehension, analysing the writer’s craft and writing analytically: ORACY: Encourage students to discuss: ‘How did you feel when you read the story? What emotions did the writer create in you as a reader?’ Students summarise the story in their own words (2-3 sentences). Activity 1: Students answer the questions on an extract from the story to develop their reading comprehension skills. Students should work independently on these questi…

learning_outcomes: I can show empathy in my writing and follow the structure of advice writing.
key_knowledge_components: To know how to demonstrate empathy, analyse character situations, and provide advice by writing an agony aunt letter in response to the novel 'Dear Nobody' by Alex Wheatle.
extracted.suggested_activities_summary: Round Robin activity. Briefly discuss the novel 'Dear Nobody' by Alex Wheatle. Explain that students will be stepping into the role of an agony aunt, providing advice and guidance to one of the characters in the novel. Oracy:discuss and analyse the character's situation, challenges, and emotions. Encourage them to consider the character's background, relationships, and personal growth throughout the story. Present the guidelines for writing an a…

learning_outcomes: I can comment on how writers use the opening of short stories to engage readers.
key_knowledge_components: To know how writers use the opening of short stories to engage readers.
key_vocab: Understanding a writer’s choices (language and form)
ORACY: Students discuss the effect of random acts of kindness. How did it make you feel? Did it change you? How important do you feel kindness is?
Take a look at paragraph one. What do you think of this as an opening to a story? Discuss with the person next to you, focusing on the structure of sentences as well as the words the writer uses.
Students consider the effectiveness of the opening of the story (structure of sentences and words the writer uses).
Activity 1: Students summarise the key elements of the story (who, what, when, where and how) and update their word bank.
Activity 2: Students complete the reading comprehension questions on the text (independently or in pairs).
Activity 3: Students write analytically about the opening of the story.
Encourage students to comment on the elements of the story they like the most in pairs.

To know how a story arc can be used for plotting short stories.

learning_outcomes: To identify headings and sub-headings in a text

extracted.suggested_activities_summary: Explain that writers also use headings and subheadings to guide the reader. ORACY: Ask students why they do this? Prompt for answers such as: Headings and sub-headings represent the key concepts and supporting ideas in the text They visually convey levels of importance Differences in text format guide the readers to distinguish the main points from the rest Headings are generally bigger, if not more conspicuous, than sub-headings

Assessment: Research Task: Find out about racism in America of the 1930s.  What was life like for African Americans? What were the Jim Crow laws?
